caveats: a: Occupation evidence is four-digit NAICS and 2023 vintage.
a: Exposure is task judgment, not observed displacement.
rho: No industry survey directly measures implemented AI opportunity.
rho: Regulatory change may accelerate digital engineering while also increasing validation burden.
e: The 109 figure is establishments, not firms.
e: The injected n is margin-bridged and may be particularly fragile for this concentrated industry.
s5: ABS owner age is cross-industry and does not observe transactions.
s5: Strategic acquisitions may dominate reported deals and are not necessarily representative of LMM availability.
q: No public evidence isolates AI-benefit pass-through.
q: Retention varies sharply between proprietary specialty vehicles and competitively bid fleet trucks.
d5: Historical output series ends in 2021.
d5: Freight tonnage does not translate one-for-one into new-truck purchases.
d5: Retail sales cover heavy-weight trucks broadly and not only domestic 336120 output.
o: Consolidation or insourcing could remove some independent operators even though physical production persists.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat production and configured-order sales of capital equipment, generally through fleet contracts, dealers, or direct commercial and public-sector procurement; revenue is product and option pricing rather than hourly service billing.
lens.excluded_activities: Large integrated OEMs outside the EBITDA band; captive plants; military armored vehicles; off-highway equipment; bodies assembled on purchased chassis; light trucks; distributors and repair-only businesses.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market establishments repeatedly manufacturing heavy-duty truck chassis, complete heavy-duty trucks, buses, heavy-duty motor homes, or special-purpose highway vehicles for external fleet, dealer, municipal, and commercial customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 336100 occupation mix rather than 336120 and does not itself measure AI exposure. I map office, engineering-documentation, scheduling, procurement, sales-support, and inspection-record tasks to partial generative-AI exposure while assigning low exposure to physical production work. Broader motor-vehicle manufacturing may overstate software-heavy engineering in small heavy-truck specialists, biasing the base upward.
caveats: Occupation evidence is four-digit NAICS and 2023 vintage.
Exposure is task judgment, not observed displacement.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 336120 establishment roster by detailed SOC combined with task-level time studies for quoting, engineering change control, production planning, purchasing, and quality documentation.
a.rationale: The broader motor-vehicle workforce includes management, business, computer, engineering, sales, office, production, maintenance, and material-moving occupations. AI can assist specifications, quoting, documentation, planning, purchasing, and quality records, but most welding, assembly, painting, testing, and material handling remain embodied work.

d5.bridge: BEA heavy-weight retail sales, BLS/FRED real sectoral output, and BTS freight forecasts measure adjacent demand/output quantities rather than constant-quality demand for LMM 336120 manufacturers. I normalize a volatile truck replacement cycle around flat-to-modest growth, with freight growth supporting the upside; sales cyclicality makes point-in-time extrapolation potentially wrong in either direction.
caveats: Historical output series ends in 2021.
Freight tonnage does not translate one-for-one into new-truck purchases.
Retail sales cover heavy-weight trucks broadly and not only domestic 336120 output.
d5.change_evidence: Latest OEM order backlogs, ACT Research or FTR Class 8 build forecasts, fleet age and replacement schedules, cancellation rates, and specialty-vehicle segment forecasts through 2031.
d5.rationale: Heavy-truck demand is cyclical: real output fell from about 99 in 2019 to 63.8 in 2020 and recovered only to 71.6 in 2021 in the cited series. Current monthly sales remain an observable demand gauge, while FAF forecasts continued long-run growth in truck-carried freight. Replacement, fleet growth, and emissions-driven product refresh support demand, offset by rates, freight cycles, and costly powertrain transitions.

caveats: The 109 figure is establishments, not firms.
The injected n is margin-bridged and may be particularly fragile for this concentrated industry.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 336120 establishment-to-enterprise crosswalk with ownership, normalized EBITDA, customer mix, certification scope, and captive-versus-external revenue flags.
e.rationale: Census reports only 109 employer establishments, and complete heavy-truck/chassis production usually requires substantial fixed capital, certification capability, and working capital. A minority of the provided LMM-count estimate is likely to be independent, transferable, recurring external suppliers rather than divisions, plants of large OEMs, or firms with economics outside the band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336120 ($3,110,340,515) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336120 ($33,868,491,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $882,337 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336120); class <5: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $707,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,928 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,389,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $215,966 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,072,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $639,324 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,984,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,897,004 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $96,371,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,711,977 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,344,305,125 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $121,525,183 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 23

caveats: Consolidation or insourcing could remove some independent operators even though physical production persists.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of contract manufacturing platforms, autonomous microfactories, or OEM insourcing that fully displaces independent lens firms rather than merely automating their tasks.
o.rationale: A physical, safety-regulated vehicle must still be engineered, assembled, tested, warranted, and delivered by an accountable manufacturer. AI and factory automation can change labor content but are unlikely to eliminate the responsible manufacturing operator within five years.

caveats: No public evidence isolates AI-benefit pass-through.
Retention varies sharply between proprietary specialty vehicles and competitively bid fleet trucks.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples showing indexation, open-book costing, productivity clauses, bid cadence, option pricing, warranty sharing, and gross-margin bridges after documented automation initiatives.
q.rationale: Configured product pricing and negotiated fleet bids allow some internal productivity benefit to remain with the manufacturer, especially between bid cycles. Sophisticated fleet buyers, competitive tenders, dealer channels, annual sourcing, and transparent component inflation will return a substantial share through lower prices or slower price increases.

caveats: No industry survey directly measures implemented AI opportunity.
Regulatory change may accelerate digital engineering while also increasing validation burden.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year automation roadmaps, realized labor-hour savings, model-error logs, and ERP/MES integration costs from several independent LMM heavy-truck or specialty-chassis manufacturers.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful in bounded back-office and engineering-support workflows, but product-safety accountability, configuration complexity, plant integration, union/change constraints, and costly errors limit autonomous deployment.

s5.bridge: Census ABS provides owner-age data for employer businesses broadly, not qualifying 336120 firms or actual five-year control transfers. I use older-owner prevalence only as succession pressure, then reduce for family/internal succession, corporate subsidiaries, failed sales, and firms without transferable standalone operations; this likely overstates transfers in an OEM-concentrated field.
caveats: ABS owner age is cross-industry and does not observe transactions.
Strategic acquisitions may dominate reported deals and are not necessarily representative of LMM availability.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial transaction counts for independent US heavy-truck and specialty-chassis manufacturers, reconciled to the eligible firm denominator and tagged for true control changes.
s5.rationale: Owner aging creates potential succession events, but the small pool, strategic/corporate ownership, capital requirements, and buyer diligence make qualifying transfers less frequent than raw retirement intent.

principal_driver: Recurring fleet replacement and freight activity support a durable need for accountable manufacturers while AI improves the information workflows around complex builds.
principal_weakness: Very limited LMM eligibility and capital-intensive physical production constrain both availability and implementable labor opportunity.
